answer: Wake him every hour overnight to confirm that he is easily arousable and to reassess for worsening headache, confusion, or vomiting; seek immediate care if any deterioration occurs
myth: You should wake someone with a concussion every hour

question: A 62-year-old man collapses while shopping at a busy mall. He is unresponsive and not breathing. Bystanders begin chest compressions, and an automated external defibrillator (AED) is attached. EMS arrives within minutes. While preparing to continue resuscitation, a new EMT asks how the AED helps in this situation. Which of the following best describes the device’s primary function during cardiac arrest?
options: Deliver an electrical shock to restart a heart that has stopped beating
Depolarize the myocardium to halt chaotic ventricular activity so an intrinsic pacemaker can reestablish an organized rhythm
Synchronize energy delivery with the R wave to safely convert atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
Increase coronary perfusion by improving preload generated during chest compressions
answer: Deliver an electrical shock to restart a heart that has stopped beating
myth: Defibrillators restart a stopped heart

question: A 24-year-old man presents to the emergency department 45 minutes after accidentally cutting his lateral calf with a new box cutter at work. He applied direct pressure, and the bleeding stopped prior to arrival. He takes no medications and has no medical history. Vital signs are normal. Examination shows a clean, 0.8-cm superficial, non-gaping linear laceration over the lateral calf without active bleeding or contamination. There is no bruit or thrill, compartments are soft, strength and sensation are intact distally, and dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are 2+ bilaterally with brisk capillary refill. Tetanus immunizations are up to date. After copious irrigation and application of a simple adhesive dressing in the ED, he asks whether he needs any tests or to stay for monitoring. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?
options: Discharge home with wound care instructions and return precautions
Obtain CT angiography of the lower extremity to evaluate for arterial injury
Observe in the emergency department for 4–6 hours with serial neurovascular examinations
Prescribe prophylactic oral antibiotics and arrange a wound check in 48 hours
answer: Discharge home with wound care instructions and return precautions
myth: If bleeding stops, the injury isn’t serious

question: A 28-year-old man comes to the emergency department 1 hour after scraping his right shin on the metal edge of a stair. He had brisk oozing that stopped after 10 minutes of direct pressure. He takes no medications and has no medical conditions. Tetanus immunizations are up to date. Vital signs are normal. Physical examination shows a 2-cm superficial linear abrasion over the mid-anterior tibia; the wound is hemostatic, not gaping, and without exposed subcutaneous fat. There is no bony tenderness or crepitus. Distal pulses are 2+ and symmetric, capillary refill is brisk, sensation is intact, and the compartments are soft. After irrigation with normal saline, the wound is dressed with a nonadherent dressing. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?
options: Discharge home with return precautions and routine wound care instructions
Obtain plain radiographs of the tibia and fibula to assess for an underlying fracture or retained foreign body
Prescribe a 7-day course of oral cephalexin for prophylaxis against skin and soft-tissue infection
Observe the patient in the emergency department for 4–6 hours to monitor for compartment syndrome
answer: Discharge home with return precautions and routine wound care instructions
myth: If bleeding stops, the injury isn’t serious

question: A 22-year-old man presents to the emergency department 2 hours after twisting his right ankle while landing from a jump during a basketball game. He has lateral ankle swelling and tenderness over the anterior talofibular ligament, intact distal pulses and sensation, and can bear weight with a limp. Ankle radiographs show no fracture. He is diagnosed with an uncomplicated inversion ankle sprain and treated with an elastic compression wrap, elevation, and NSAIDs. Which of the following discharge instructions regarding cold therapy is most appropriate for the next 48 hours?
options: Apply crushed ice directly to the lateral ankle for 15–20 minutes at a time every 2–3 hours while awake
Avoid any cold application for the first 24 hours, then begin icing on day 2
Use warm, moist heat for 20 minutes every 2–3 hours during the first 24 hours to improve perfusion
Apply a cold pack continuously for 45–60 minutes twice daily to maximize swelling reduction
answer: Apply crushed ice directly to the lateral ankle for 15–20 minutes at a time every 2–3 hours while awake
myth: Ice should be applied directly to skin for injuries

question: A 16-year-old boy presents to the emergency department 4 hours after a head-to-head collision during a soccer match. He had a brief loss of consciousness lasting about 15 seconds and now reports a mild, diffuse headache and slight nausea without vomiting. He has no history of bleeding disorders and takes no medications. Vital signs are normal. He is alert and oriented with a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 15. Neurologic examination is normal, and there are no signs of basilar skull fracture. He is observed in the ED for several hours with stable symptoms and is deemed safe for discharge with his mother. She asks how to monitor him overnight. Which of the following discharge instructions is most appropriate?
options: Have a responsible adult wake him every hour overnight to ensure he is easily arousable and appropriately oriented, and return if symptoms worsen
Allow uninterrupted sleep without any checks overnight as long as his symptoms remain unchanged
Use ibuprofen or aspirin as needed for headache and plan to check on him in the morning
If he feels better tomorrow, he may resume sports as tolerated, including practice
answer: Have a responsible adult wake him every hour overnight to ensure he is easily arousable and appropriately oriented, and return if symptoms worsen
myth: You should wake someone with a concussion every hour

question: A 28-year-old man falls approximately 15 feet from a ladder onto concrete at a construction site. He is awake and oriented, complaining of severe posterior neck pain and tingling in both hands. He is breathing comfortably with a pulse of 98/min. There is no obvious external bleeding, and the area is calm with no ongoing hazards. You are the first person to reach him while others contact emergency services. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Move the patient immediately to a nearby flat surface to prevent shock and reduce the risk of further injury.
Avoid moving the patient; manually stabilize the head and neck in the position found and monitor airway and breathing until emergency services arrive.
Sit the patient up to better assess for dizziness and level of consciousness while awaiting emergency services.
Elevate the patient’s legs and offer small sips of water to prevent hypotension while reassuring him.
answer: Move the patient immediately to a nearby flat surface to prevent shock and reduce the risk of further injury.
myth: You must move someone immediately after a serious fall or crash

question: A 35-year-old construction worker falls approximately 6 meters (20 feet) from scaffolding onto gravel. You are the first person to reach him. He is awake and speaking in full sentences, complaining of severe neck pain and tingling in both hands. He is lying supine on uneven ground. There is no active external bleeding, no fire, and no apparent environmental hazard. Vital signs: pulse 96/min, respirations 18/min, blood pressure 128/78 mm Hg, oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Coworkers ask what to do next. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: With assistance, carefully move the patient onto a flat, firm surface immediately before further assessment
Keep the patient still with manual in-line stabilization of the head and neck and monitor airway, breathing, and circulation until EMS arrives
Ask the patient to slowly sit upright to assess motor function and stability of the spine
Provide oral fluids and over-the-counter analgesics to prevent dehydration and reduce pain while awaiting EMS
answer: With assistance, carefully move the patient onto a flat, firm surface immediately before further assessment
myth: You must move someone immediately after a serious fall or crash

question: A 22-year-old man presents to the emergency department 1 hour after cutting the dorsal aspect of his forearm on the clean edge of a metal shelf at work. He applied direct pressure and reports that the bleeding stopped. He has minimal pain and denies numbness or weakness. He takes no medications, and his tetanus immunizations are up to date. Vital signs are normal. Examination shows a 0.8-cm linear, superficial laceration on the mid-dorsal forearm that is hemostatic with well-approximated edges and no gaping, no visible foreign material, and no exposed tendon. Sensation is intact in the radial, ulnar, and median nerve distributions; strength and active range of motion at the wrist and fingers are normal; capillary refill is brisk and the radial pulse is 2+. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Irrigate the wound, apply topical antibiotic ointment and a dry dressing, and discharge with routine wound care instructions; no closure or imaging is indicated for a small, hemostatic superficial laceration with a normal neurovascular exam
Close the wound with simple interrupted sutures and arrange suture removal in 7 days to optimize cosmesis and reduce infection risk
Obtain plain radiographs of the forearm to assess for a retained foreign body before proceeding with wound management
Prescribe a 5-day course of oral cephalexin because the laceration involved contact with a metal surface
answer: Irrigate the wound, apply topical antibiotic ointment and a dry dressing, and discharge with routine wound care instructions; no closure or imaging is indicated for a small, hemostatic superficial laceration with a normal neurovascular exam
myth: If bleeding stops, the injury isn’t serious

question: A 29-year-old man falls approximately 4 meters (13 feet) from scaffolding at a construction site. He is found supine, awake, and oriented but in severe neck and upper back pain. He reports tingling in both hands. Respirations are regular, and there is no obvious external bleeding. You are the first medically trained person on scene. Emergency medical services have been called and are estimated to arrive in 8 minutes. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Carefully move the patient to a nearby flat, open area before further assessment to prevent additional injury
Instruct the patient not to move, manually maintain in-line cervical spine stabilization, and assess airway, breathing, and circulation until EMS arrives
Place the patient in a seated position to better evaluate neurologic deficits
Apply a makeshift cervical collar with a rolled towel and otherwise avoid touching the patient until EMS arrives
answer: Carefully move the patient to a nearby flat, open area before further assessment to prevent additional injury
myth: You must move someone immediately after a serious fall or crash

question: A 34-year-old construction worker falls 12 feet from scaffolding onto compacted dirt. He is conscious, lying supine, and complains of severe neck and upper back pain with tingling in both hands. He has no obvious external bleeding and is breathing comfortably. A coworker has called 911; the estimated ambulance arrival is 10 minutes. The weather is cold, but there is no fire, traffic hazard, or other immediate environmental danger. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step by the coworkers while waiting for EMS?
options: Carefully move the patient immediately to a nearby truck to transport him to the emergency department, supporting his head and neck as best as possible
Instruct the patient not to move, manually stabilize his cervical spine in-line, monitor airway and breathing, and wait for EMS
Assist the patient into a seated position to assess for dizziness and to make breathing more comfortable
Offer the patient oral fluids and an over-the-counter analgesic, and cover him with a blanket
answer: Carefully move the patient immediately to a nearby truck to transport him to the emergency department, supporting his head and neck as best as possible
myth: You must move someone immediately after a serious fall or crash

question: A 62-year-old man collapses at home and is brought to the emergency department by EMS. On arrival, he is unresponsive, apneic, and pulseless. Cardiac monitoring shows a flatline tracing confirmed in two leads. Large-bore IV access is established, and bag-valve-mask ventilations with 100% oxygen are being provided. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Deliver immediate biphasic defibrillation at 200 J
Initiate high-quality chest compressions and administer epinephrine 1 mg IV push
Perform endotracheal intubation before initiating chest compressions
Obtain a 12-lead ECG prior to further intervention
answer: Deliver immediate biphasic defibrillation at 200 J
myth: Defibrillators restart a stopped heart

question: A 27-year-old right-handed hiker is bitten on the ulnar aspect of the left hand while clearing brush in a wooded area of central Florida. The snake is described as small and slender with red, yellow, and black bands in which the red and yellow bands touch. On arrival to a trailhead 15 minutes later, he has two small puncture wounds with minimal pain and swelling, intact distal pulses, and normal vital signs. His companion has removed rings, kept the limb still, and splinted it at the level of the heart. EMS has been contacted, with an estimated arrival of 60–90 minutes due to the remote location. A basic first-aid kit with an elastic bandage and gauze is available. To limit systemic spread of venom while awaiting EMS, which on-site action is most appropriate?
options: Apply a tight proximal tourniquet sufficient to eliminate the radial pulse
Use the mouth to apply direct suction over the puncture wounds to remove venom
Apply a firm pressure immobilization bandage to the entire limb and maintain splinting
Attempt to locate and capture the snake for identification and transport it to the hospital
answer: Use the mouth to apply direct suction over the puncture wounds to remove venom
myth: You should suck venom out of a snakebite

question: A 20-year-old man presents to the emergency department 1 hour after twisting his left ankle while playing basketball. He has swelling and point tenderness over the anterior talofibular ligament. He can bear weight with a mild limp. Neurovascular status is intact, and ankle radiographs show no fracture. In addition to rest, elevation, and an elastic compression wrap, which of the following discharge instructions is most appropriate to reduce pain and swelling over the next 24–48 hours?
options: Perform ice massage by rubbing an ice cube directly on the skin over the most tender area for about 10 minutes every 2–3 hours while awake
Apply a heating pad over the ankle for 15–20 minutes every 2–3 hours during the first 24 hours to increase circulation
Begin warm–cold contrast baths within the first several hours after injury to promote alternating vasodilation and vasoconstriction
Avoid all weight bearing and keep the ankle immobilized in a rigid splint for 7–10 days regardless of symptom improvement
answer: Perform ice massage by rubbing an ice cube directly on the skin over the most tender area for about 10 minutes every 2–3 hours while awake
myth: Ice should be applied directly to skin for injuries

question: A 36-year-old man in the emergency department waiting area begins coughing forcefully immediately after taking a bite of a sandwich. He is able to answer questions in full sentences and has a strong, effective cough. There is no stridor, drooling, or voice change, and he appears comfortable. Vital signs are temperature 36.8 C, blood pressure 126/78 mm Hg, heart rate 92/min, respiratory rate 16/min, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Encourage continued coughing and closely observe until symptoms resolve
Perform immediate abdominal thrusts
Provide reassurance and allow routine waiting-room observation without further action
Arrange emergent laryngoscopy to remove a suspected airway foreign body
answer: Provide reassurance and allow routine waiting-room observation without further action
myth: If someone can talk, they’re not choking

question: A 68-year-old man with hypertension and hyperlipidemia collapses at home. EMS arrives within 5 minutes and initiates high-quality chest compressions with bag-mask ventilation. On arrival to the emergency department, he remains pulseless and asystolic, and advanced life support is continued. His spouse, who has seen CPR on television, anxiously asks what to expect from the resuscitation. Which of the following statements best reflects a common public expectation about CPR outcomes that should be addressed during counseling?
options: If started promptly, chest compressions typically restore a normal heartbeat right away, and most patients wake up immediately.
Even with prompt, high-quality CPR, many patients do not regain a pulse, and when circulation returns it may be delayed and neurologic recovery is uncertain.
High-quality chest compressions at a rate of 100–120/min and depth of 5–6 cm provide partial cardiac output to sustain vital organs until definitive therapy is given.
Defibrillation is primarily effective for shockable rhythms such as ventricular fibrillation or pulseless ventricular tachycardia and is not useful in asystole.
answer: If started promptly, chest compressions typically restore a normal heartbeat right away, and most patients wake up immediately.
myth: CPR always brings people back to life immediately

question: A 22-year-old man presents to the emergency department 1 hour after twisting his right ankle while playing basketball. He has swelling and pain over the lateral ankle and is able to bear weight with a limp. Vital signs are normal. Examination shows lateral swelling and tenderness over the anterior talofibular ligament without deformity; the overlying skin is intact, and sensation and distal pulses are normal. There is no bony tenderness over the posterior edge or tip of either malleolus, the navicular, or the base of the fifth metatarsal. Ankle radiographs show no fracture. He is discharged with crutches and an elastic wrap, and you review protection, rest, compression, and elevation. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate home measure to reduce pain and swelling?
options: Perform ice massage to the lateral ankle for 10–15 minutes every 2–3 hours during the first 48 hours
Apply a heating pad to the ankle for 20 minutes every 2–3 hours during the first 24 hours
Avoid cold therapy during the first 24 hours; begin icing only after swelling starts to subside
Begin alternating hot- and cold-water immersion of the ankle immediately for 15 minutes at a time
answer: Perform ice massage to the lateral ankle for 10–15 minutes every 2–3 hours during the first 48 hours
myth: Ice should be applied directly to skin for injuries

question: A 64-year-old man with hypertension collapses at home. His spouse calls emergency services and begins chest compressions as instructed by the dispatcher. Paramedics arrive 6 minutes later and continue high-quality CPR; an AED advises shock for ventricular fibrillation, and two shocks are delivered with transient organized rhythm that deteriorates to pulseless electrical activity. He arrives to the emergency department 12 minutes after collapse with ongoing ventilatory support and chest compressions. The patient remains unresponsive and pulseless. His adult daughter asks, "Will the CPR bring him back right now?" Which of the following is the most appropriate statement for the physician to make regarding the immediate effect of CPR?
options: When performed correctly and started promptly, CPR typically restarts the heart and leads to an immediate return of consciousness in most adults with sudden cardiac arrest.
High-quality CPR mainly provides limited blood flow to vital organs and improves the chance of return of spontaneous circulation, but immediate awakening is uncommon even with optimal care.
In witnessed ventricular fibrillation arrest, intravenous medications are usually more effective than early defibrillation in producing rapid return of spontaneous circulation.
Immediate endotracheal intubation is the key intervention that typically reverses cardiac arrest and restores consciousness more quickly than chest compressions or defibrillation.
answer: When performed correctly and started promptly, CPR typically restarts the heart and leads to an immediate return of consciousness in most adults with sudden cardiac arrest.
myth: CPR always brings people back to life immediately

question: A 26-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with abrupt onset of palpitations, chest tightness, shortness of breath, trembling, and a sense of impending doom that began 20 minutes ago during a stressful work meeting. She reports two similar episodes in the past month that resolved within 15–20 minutes without treatment. She takes no medications, does not smoke, and denies alcohol or illicit drug use. She is not on hormonal contraception and has had no recent travel, surgery, or immobilization. Temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), blood pressure is 124/74 mm Hg, pulse is 100/min, respirations are 22/min, and oxygen saturation is 99% on room air. Cardiac and pulmonary examinations are normal. A 12-lead ECG shows normal sinus rhythm without ischemic changes. After 30 minutes of quiet observation and coached breathing, her symptoms have largely abated; she declines medication and says she feels ready to go home. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Provide brief reassurance that this is a self-limited stress reaction; no further diagnostic testing or referral is necessary, and she may be discharged with routine return precautions
Educate the patient about panic attacks and arrange outpatient cognitive-behavioral therapy and primary care follow-up to evaluate for panic disorder
Administer a benzodiazepine now and prescribe a short course for as-needed use after discharge
Obtain a D-dimer test to evaluate for pulmonary embolism given her tachycardia and dyspnea
answer: Provide brief reassurance that this is a self-limited stress reaction; no further diagnostic testing or referral is necessary, and she may be discharged with routine return precautions
myth: A person having a panic attack is just overreacting

question: A 25-year-old woman with no significant medical history is brought to the emergency department 15 minutes after sudden onset of chest tightness, palpitations, shortness of breath, lightheadedness, and tingling in her fingers while studying for an upcoming licensing exam. She describes an intense sense of dread that something terrible was happening. She does not smoke and takes no medications. Temperature is 36.9°C (98.4°F), blood pressure is 124/74 mm Hg, pulse is 108/min, respirations are 24/min, and oxygen saturation is 99% on room air. Cardiac and pulmonary examinations are normal. ECG shows sinus tachycardia without ischemic changes. High-sensitivity troponin is within the reference range. Her symptoms improve with quiet reassurance and coached breathing over the next 20 minutes. She asks, “Is something seriously wrong with my heart or lungs?” Which of the following is the most appropriate explanation to provide now?
options: Your symptoms are due to a stress reaction rather than a medical disorder of the heart or lungs; reassurance is sufficient and no further evaluation is needed
This presentation is most consistent with generalized anxiety disorder; begin a daily selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor now for long-term control
Given the acute dyspnea, proceed with CT pulmonary angiography to evaluate for pulmonary embolism despite the reassuring examination and ECG
Atypical acute coronary syndrome remains a likely cause; admit for serial troponin testing and continuous telemetry monitoring
answer: Your symptoms are due to a stress reaction rather than a medical disorder of the heart or lungs; reassurance is sufficient and no further evaluation is needed
myth: A person having a panic attack is just overreacting

question: A 64-year-old man with hypertension collapses at home while talking with his spouse. She immediately calls EMS and begins chest compressions following dispatcher instructions. Paramedics arrive within 6 minutes and continue high-quality CPR with bag-valve-mask ventilation en route to the emergency department. On arrival, he remains pulseless. The spouse, visibly distressed, asks the physician, “When CPR is started right away like this, doesn’t it usually bring someone back right away?” Which of the following is the most accurate response to provide about the immediate effects of timely, high-quality CPR?
options: When started promptly and performed correctly, CPR generally results in rapid return of pulse and consciousness in most adults with witnessed cardiac arrest.
Effective CPR primarily provides temporary perfusion to vital organs; immediate restoration of circulation is uncommon even when compressions are high quality and started early.
Because prolonged efforts are harmful, chest compressions should be stopped after 2 minutes if no pulse is detected to reassess goals of care.
Early placement of an endotracheal tube takes precedence over uninterrupted chest compressions during the first minutes of cardiac arrest.
answer: When started promptly and performed correctly, CPR generally results in rapid return of pulse and consciousness in most adults with witnessed cardiac arrest.
myth: CPR always brings people back to life immediately

question: A 24-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after a motorcycle crash. He is awake and oriented and answers questions calmly without visible tremor. He reports abdominal pain and lightheadedness. Vital signs are blood pressure 88/54 mm Hg, heart rate 128/min, respiratory rate 24/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. His skin is cool and clammy, and capillary refill is 4 seconds. The junior resident tells the attending, “I don’t think he’s in shock.” Which of the following observations most likely led the resident to that conclusion?
options: The patient appears calm and is not visibly shaking
Persistent hypotension with tachycardia
Cool, clammy extremities with delayed capillary refill
Pallor and lightheadedness following trauma
answer: The patient appears calm and is not visibly shaking
myth: Shock means someone has to be visibly panicking or shaking

question: A 64-year-old man with hypertension collapses at home while eating dinner. His spouse calls emergency services immediately and begins dispatcher-assisted chest compressions within 2 minutes. EMS arrives 5 minutes later, finds him pulseless, and continues high-quality CPR with bag-mask ventilation. An AED initially advises a shock for ventricular fibrillation, which is delivered. He is transported to the emergency department, where resuscitation continues. The spouse, visibly distraught, asks the physician, "When will he come back now that you're doing CPR?" Which of the following is the most appropriate expectation to communicate about the immediate effects of CPR?
options: Effective chest compressions generally restart the heart right away, and patients typically regain consciousness within minutes.
Chest compressions primarily provide temporary circulation; even with prompt, high-quality CPR and defibrillation, many patients do not regain a pulse or consciousness immediately.
CPR should be delayed until an advanced airway is secured to minimize the risk of regurgitation and aspiration.
Because the initial rhythm was ventricular fibrillation, resuscitation should be terminated after a single shock if there is no immediate awakening.
answer: Effective chest compressions generally restart the heart right away, and patients typically regain consciousness within minutes.
myth: CPR always brings people back to life immediately

question: A 66-year-old man presents to the emergency department with dizziness and two episodes of melena over the past day. He is pale and diaphoretic. Blood pressure is 86/52 mm Hg, pulse is 118/min, respiratory rate is 22/min, and oxygen saturation is 97% on room air. He is alert and oriented and answers questions appropriately. Which of the following additional findings, if present, would most strongly argue against a diagnosis of shock in this patient?
options: Calm affect without agitation or tremulousness
Serum lactate concentration of 5.2 mmol/L
Cool, clammy extremities with delayed capillary refill
Narrow pulse pressure with persistent tachycardia
answer: Calm affect without agitation or tremulousness
myth: Shock means someone has to be visibly panicking or shaking

question: A 22-year-old man presents to the emergency department 2 hours after inverting his left ankle while playing soccer. He was able to limp off the field. Examination shows swelling and tenderness over the anterior talofibular ligament with mild ecchymosis; there is no bony tenderness over the posterior edge or tip of the malleoli, and he can bear weight with pain. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are intact, and sensation is normal. Ankle radiographs show no fracture. A functional brace and elastic compression wrap are applied, and he is provided crutches and oral analgesics. Which of the following discharge instructions is most appropriate to reduce pain and swelling over the next 48 hours?
options: Apply an ice pack to the skin over the lateral ankle for 15–20 minutes every 2–3 hours while awake during the first 48 hours and keep the ankle elevated when resting
Begin warm moist heat to the ankle for 15–20 minutes, 3–4 times daily starting today to promote circulation and reduce swelling
Avoid cold therapy because it can impair tissue perfusion; rely on compression alone and resume activity as tolerated
Remain strictly non–weight-bearing in a posterior splint for 5–7 days; avoid range-of-motion exercises until pain has fully resolved
answer: Apply an ice pack to the skin over the lateral ankle for 15–20 minutes every 2–3 hours while awake during the first 48 hours and keep the ankle elevated when resting
myth: Ice should be applied directly to skin for injuries

question: A 58-year-old man collapses in a crowded train station. He is unresponsive, has agonal respirations, and no palpable carotid pulse. A trained bystander begins high-quality chest compressions while another applies an automated external defibrillator (AED). The device analyzes the rhythm and advises a shock, which is delivered; chest compressions are immediately resumed. In this out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, the primary therapeutic purpose of the unsynchronized shock is to:
options: Reinitiate organized cardiac electrical and mechanical activity in a pulseless patient by delivering a transthoracic shock
Improve coronary and cerebral perfusion primarily through augmentation of diastolic pressure generated by chest compressions
Deliver synchronized cardioversion for a narrow-complex supraventricular tachyarrhythmia
Provide external pacing to treat profound symptomatic bradycardia until intrinsic rhythm returns
answer: Reinitiate organized cardiac electrical and mechanical activity in a pulseless patient by delivering a transthoracic shock
myth: Defibrillators restart a stopped heart

question: A 28-year-old man falls 12 feet from scaffolding onto packed earth at a construction site. He is found supine, alert, and oriented and reports severe posterior neck pain with tingling in both hands. Respiratory rate is 16/min with unlabored breathing; pulse is 92/min; blood pressure is 128/76 mm Hg; and oxygen saturation is 98% on room air. There is no visible external bleeding. The scene is secure, and a rigid spine board with straps is available. Emergency medical services are estimated to arrive in 6 minutes. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: With manual in-line stabilization, perform a log-roll and transfer the patient immediately to a rigid spine board in the supine position for transport
Apply a soft cervical collar, support the head with pillows, and perform a detailed neurologic examination before any movement
Allow the patient to sit upright for comfort and administer oral analgesics while awaiting EMS arrival
Remove outer clothing and footwear to inspect for additional injuries and external bleeding before deciding on movement
answer: With manual in-line stabilization, perform a log-roll and transfer the patient immediately to a rigid spine board in the supine position for transport
myth: You must move someone immediately after a serious fall or crash

question: A 55-year-old man presents to the emergency department with 2 days of profuse vomiting and watery diarrhea. He reports lightheadedness when standing. Vital signs are temperature 37.0°C, blood pressure 88/54 mm Hg, heart rate 122/min, respiratory rate 22/min, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. Physical examination reveals dry mucous membranes. Which of the following additional findings, if present, would most argue against the diagnosis of shock in this patient?
options: No acute distress on examination
Narrow pulse pressure in the setting of tachycardia
Oliguria with a rising serum creatinine
Elevated serum lactate level
answer: No acute distress on examination
myth: Shock means someone has to be visibly panicking or shaking

question: During a multi-vehicle collision with dozens of victims, an emergency physician assigns you to perform an initial visual triage sweep to flag patients who may be in shock and should be prioritized for immediate hands-on assessment once resources free up. Based solely on what you can see at a glance, which patient would you flag as most likely in shock?
options: A young woman clutching her abdomen who is visibly panicking, trembling, and shaking, breathing rapidly, and repeatedly saying she is going to pass out
A middle-aged man sitting upright, conversing clearly on his phone with normal-appearing skin color and no apparent distress
A teenage boy ambulating with a steady gait, holding pressure over a forearm laceration while joking with a friend
An older woman reclining on a stretcher with eyes closed, breathing evenly, pink dry skin, and opening her eyes to answer questions appropriately when addressed
answer: A young woman clutching her abdomen who is visibly panicking, trembling, and shaking, breathing rapidly, and repeatedly saying she is going to pass out
myth: Shock means someone has to be visibly panicking or shaking

question: A 64-year-old man with hypertension collapses while mowing the lawn. His wife calls emergency medical services immediately and begins dispatcher-assisted chest compressions. Paramedics arrive 5 minutes later, find ventricular fibrillation, deliver a shock, and continue high-quality CPR en route to the emergency department. In the ED, resuscitation efforts are ongoing. Which of the following statements by the spouse would most strongly indicate a common misunderstanding about the immediate effects of high-quality CPR and early defibrillation that should be addressed?
options: “Once you start chest compressions and shock his heart, he should get a pulse and wake up right away, right?”
“I know you’re keeping blood moving with compressions, but he might not wake up even if you get his heart going again.”
“Please do whatever you can quickly—I’m okay if you have to shock him early before you get an IV started.”
“If he starts gasping, I realize that might just be part of the arrest and not mean he’s better yet.”
answer: “Once you start chest compressions and shock his heart, he should get a pulse and wake up right away, right?”
myth: CPR always brings people back to life immediately

question: A 27-year-old man is brought to the emergency department 20 minutes after being struck by a car while riding a bicycle. He is alert and conversant. Vital signs are blood pressure 104/68 mm Hg, pulse 108/min, respirations 20/min, temperature 36.8°C (98.2°F), and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. He has a 6-cm laceration of the left thigh with bleeding controlled by direct pressure. Two large-bore IV catheters are placed, and laboratory studies are pending. The team plans serial reassessments to monitor for evolving hemorrhagic shock over the next hour. Which of the following findings, if noted on reassessment, would most argue against the presence of shock?
options: Relaxed affect without agitation or tremulousness
Cool, clammy extremities with delayed capillary refill
New disorientation to time and place
Urine output decreasing to <0.5 mL/kg/hr via Foley catheter
answer: Relaxed affect without agitation or tremulousness
myth: Shock means someone has to be visibly panicking or shaking
